title: Impact of the British Rule on India
subject: history
highYieldFacts: (a) Lord Cornwallis introduced a new land settlement called the Permanent Settlement; (b) Thomas Munro, the Governor of Madras (1820–27), introduced the Ryotwari Settlement.

Towards the end of the 18th century, the EEIC was no longer a mere trading company. It had been transformed into a formidable colonial power controlling most of India. In order to retain control over their colony, the British established a system of administration for the maintenance of law and order, as well as for internal security.

Indian agriculture received special attention from the EEIC. This was primarily because the main source of the state income was land revenue. Moreover, it was the aim of the British government to establish India as an agricultural supplier of cheap raw materials to industries in England. The Company tried various experiments to maximise the land revenue, regardless of its oppressive impact on Indian peasants. Some of these land revenue policies were as follows:
■ Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement as a system of revenue collection in 1793 in Bengal, Bihar and Orissa.
The land revenue settlements, regardless of the region of implementation, had certain common features. These were as follows:
Creation of a new social class of landlords who were loyal to the British
The Company was assured a fixed revenue or income without the trouble of collecting it from the peasants.

The early 10th century, the British Government passed the Charter Act of 1812, industry the Company was required to spend the last rupees annually on the education of Indians. The Charter Act of 1813 sparked off a debate on education across England as people argued about what policy should he implemented to educate the people of India. One group argued that the traditional learning should be imparted in classical and regional Indian languages. These were the Orientalists. On the other hand, the Anglicists, led by Thomas Macaulay, insisted that the medium of instruction should be English and the education imparted should be Western.
Lord William Beetwick finally put an end to the debate on education by passing a conclusion that defined the education policy implemented by the British in India.
Governor-Governor-Governor-Governor-Governor-G
This class was also cheap human resources the administration.

Indian industries also suffered a great setback under the British colonial rule. The British government, in the early years of the 18th century, imposed heavy duties on Indian textile imports to Britain. On the other hand, British machine-made goods entered India duty-free or at a nominal cost. The introduction of such a policy of one-way free trade made Indian goods very costly. It resulted in a significant decrease in sale for the Indian artisans and loss of their livelihoods.
##### Transport and Communication

The British realised that to consolidate their position in India, the development of proper communication facilities was necessary. They built an elaborate network of roads, canals and railway lines to connect all major cities, ports and agricultural centres. Metalled roads were built, and the railway system was introduced.
Then In 1853, the first railway line from Bombay to Thane was inaugurated. In the same year, the post and telegraph department was set up. The first telegraph line was opened from Calcutta to Agra.
One of the earliest trains in India, travelling from Bombay to Thane in 1855
critical thinking: comparative analysis, reasoning information literacy: general awareness
The British government drained the Indian economy and exploited it to the maximum. The exploitation took the following forms:

The British bought raw materials at cheap prices and sold their manufactured goods at high prices, flooding the Indian markets with their products. This led to the ruin of small-scale cottage industries.
The peasants were forced to pay high taxes, leading to increase in the poverty level and discontent. Therefore, a number of peasant revolts and uprisings took place.

In the early 19th century, the British government passed the Charter Act of 1813, whereby the Company was required to spend one lakh rupees annually on the education of Indians. The Charter Act of 1813 sparked off a debate on education across England as people argued about what policy should be implemented to educate the people of India. One group argued that the traditional learning should be imparted in classical and regional Indian languages. These were the Orientalists. On the other hand, the Anglicists, led by Thomas Macaulay, insisted that the medium of instruction should be English and the education imparted should be Western.

notes.subtopic: 2. ECONOMIC IMPACT OF THE BRITISH RULE
notes.content:
**Executive summary:** Towards the end of the 18th century, the EEIC was no longer a mere trading company. It had been transformed into a formidable colonial power controlling most of India. In order to 

**Must know**
• Towards the end of the 18th century, the EEIC was no longer a mere trading company. It had been transformed into a formidable colonial power controlling most of India. In order to retain control over their colony, the British established a system of administration for the maintenance of law and order, as well as for internal security.
• Indian agriculture received special attention from the EEIC. This was primarily because the main source of the state income was land revenue. Moreover, it was the aim of the British government to establish India as an agricultural supplier of cheap raw materials to industries in England. The Company tried various experiments to maximise the land revenue, regardless of its oppressive impact on Indian peasants. Some of these land revenue policies were as follows:
• ■ Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement as a system of revenue collection in 1793 in Bengal, Bihar and Orissa.
• The Ryotwari Settlement was introduced in Bombay and Madras Presidencies.
• The Mahalwari Settlement was introduced in the northern part of the country, mainly in Uttar Pradesh and Punjab, where it had devastating effects.

Towards the end of the 18th century, the EEIC was no longer a mere trading company. It had been transformed into a formidable colonial power controlling most of India. In order to retain control over their colony, the British established a system of administration for the maintenance of law and order, as well as for internal security.

Indian agriculture received special attention from the EEIC. This was primarily because the main source of the state income was land revenue. Moreover, it was the aim of the British government to establish India as an agricultural supplier of cheap raw materials to industries in England. The Company tried various experiments to maximise the land revenue, regardless of its oppressive impact on Indian peasants. Some of these land revenue policies were as follows:

■ Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement as a system of revenue collection in 1793 in Bengal, Bihar and Orissa.

The Ryotwari Settlement was introduced in Bombay and Madras Presidencies.

The Mahalwari Settlement was introduced in the northern part of the country, mainly in Uttar Pradesh and Punjab, where it had devastating effects.

**Executive summary:** Indian industries also suffered a great setback under the British colonial rule. The British government, in the early years of the 18th century, imposed heavy duties on Indian text

**Must know**
• Indian industries also suffered a great setback under the British colonial rule. The British government, in the early years of the 18th century, imposed heavy duties on Indian textile imports to Britain. On the other hand, British machine-made goods entered India duty-free or at a nominal cost. The introduction of such a policy of one-way free trade made Indian goods very costly. It resulted in a significant decrease in sale for the Indian artisans and loss of their livelihoods.
• ##### Transport and Communication
• The British realised that to consolidate their position in India, the development of proper communication facilities was necessary. They built an elaborate network of roads, canals and railway lines to connect all major cities, ports and agricultural centres. Metalled roads were built, and the railway system was introduced.
• ##### Benefits of Improved Transport System
• The improved transport system ensured more efficient governance for the British. They could quickly transport troops and rapidly communicate to quell revolts and other disturbances.
• It became easier to transfer raw materials and finished goods for the promotion of trade and commerce.

Indian industries also suffered a great setback under the British colonial rule. ##### Transport and Communication

The British realised that to consolidate their position in India, the development of proper communication facilities was necessary. They built an elaborate network of roads, canals and railway lines to connect all major cities, ports and agricultural centres. Metalled roads were built, and the railway system was introduced.

##### Benefits of Improved Transport System

The improved transport system ensured more efficient governance for the British. They could quickly transport troops and rapidly communicate to quell revolts and other disturbances.

It became easier to transfer raw materials and finished goods for the promotion of trade and commerce.

The postal department in India has also grown in leaps and bounds since independence. Presently, there are more than 1.5 lakh post offices that are in operation all over India. The Government of India is actively involved in modernising the transport and communication system in India, while retaining its affordability.

notes.content:
**Executive summary:** The British government drained the Indian economy and exploited it to the maximum. The exploitation took the following forms:

**Must know**
• The British government drained the Indian economy and exploited it to the maximum. The exploitation took the following forms:
• The British bought raw materials at cheap prices and sold their manufactured goods at high prices, flooding the Indian markets with their products. This led to the ruin of small-scale cottage industries.
• The peasants were forced to pay high taxes, leading to increase in the poverty level and discontent. Therefore, a number of peasant revolts and uprisings took place.
• The workers were paid low wages. The British made no effort to improve industry or agriculture in India.
• India's wealth was being systematically drained away to England.

The British government drained the Indian economy and exploited it to the maximum. The exploitation took the following forms:

The British bought raw materials at cheap prices and sold their manufactured goods at high prices, flooding the Indian markets with their products. This led to the ruin of small-scale cottage industries.

The peasants were forced to pay high taxes, leading to increase in the poverty level and discontent. Therefore, a number of peasant revolts and uprisings took place.

The workers were paid low wages. The British made no effort to improve industry or agriculture in India.

India's wealth was being systematically drained away to England.

It resulted in a significant decrease in sale for the Indian artisans and loss of their livelihoods.

questions.question:

The British realised that to consolidate their position in India, the development of proper communication facilities was...
options: The British realised that to consolidate their position in India, the development of proper communic
It applies to a different period.
questions.answer: The British realised that to consolidate their position in India, the development of proper communication facilities was necessary. They built an elaborate network of roads, canals and railway lines to connect all major cities, ports and agricultural centres. Metalled roads were built, and the railway system was introduced.

questions.question:
Which year is associated with the following event/fact?
In the early 19th century, the British government passed the Charter Act of 1813, whereby the Company was required to spend one lakh rupees annually o
questions.answer: The correct year is **1813**. In the early 19th century, the British government passed the Charter Act of 1813, whereby the Company was required to spend one lakh rupees annually on the education of Indians. The Charter Act of 1813 sparked off a debate on education across England as people argued about what policy should be implemented to educate the people of India. One group argued that the traditional learning should be imparted in classical and regional Indian languages. These were the Orientalists. On the other hand, the Anglicists, led by Thomas Macaulay, insisted that the medium of instruction should be English and the education imparted should be Western.

questions.question: The term '______' means: when a person owns a piece of property and rents it out for profit, but does not live within the loc
questions.answer: **Absentee landlordism** — when a person owns a piece of property and rents it out for profit, but does not live within the local region of the property

questions.question: The term '______' means: those who felt that education imparted to the Indians should be Western, and the medium of instructi
questions.answer: **Anglicists** — those who felt that education imparted to the Indians should be Western, and the medium of instruction should be English

questions.question: The term '______' means: inspiring fear or respect through use of power
questions.answer: **Formidable** — inspiring fear or respect through use of power

questions.question: The term '______' means: extension of rule or authority over a foreign nation
questions.answer: **Imperialist** — extension of rule or authority over a foreign nation

questions.question: The term '______' means: relating to those who are inspired by ideas of liberty and equality to work for a united nation
questions.answer: **Nationalist** — relating to those who are inspired by ideas of liberty and equality to work for a united nation

questions.answer: Negative Effects of Western Education → Positive Effects of Western Education

questions.question: Match Column I with Column II (2):

pairs.left: Led to complete Westernisation of the educational system
pairs.right: Created awareness among Indians about being subjected to a foreign rule

questions.answer: Led to complete Westernisation of the educational system → Created awareness among Indians about being subjected to a foreign rule

questions.question: Match Column I with Column II (3):

pairs.left: Caused replacement of the indigenous methods of education
pairs.right: Resulted in the growth of the ideas on democracy, liberty and fraternity among educated Indians

questions.answer: Caused replacement of the indigenous methods of education → Resulted in the growth of the ideas on democracy, liberty and fraternity among educated Indians

questions.question: Match Column I with Column II (4):
questions.answer: Land Revenue Policy → Region

questions.question: Match Column I with Column II (5):

questions.answer: Ryotwari Settlement → ?

pairs.right: northern part of the country, mainly in Uttar Pradesh and Punjab

questions.answer: Mahalwari Settlement → northern part of the country, mainly in Uttar Pradesh and Punjab

questions.question: What steps were taken by the British to improve transport in India?
questions.answer:
• (a) Lord Cornwallis introduced a new land settlement called the Permanent Settlement; (b) Thomas Munro, the Governor of Madras (1820–27), introduced the Ryotwari Settlement.
• Towards the end of the 18th century, the EEIC was no longer a mere trading company. It had been transformed into a formidable colonial power controlling most of India. In order to retain control over their colony, the British established a system of administration for the maintenance of law and order, as well as for internal security.
• Indian agriculture received special attention from the EEIC. This was primarily because the main source of the state income was land revenue. Moreover, it was the aim of the British government to establish India as an agricultural supplier of cheap raw materials to industries in England. The Company tried various experiments to maximise the land revenue, regardless of its oppressive impact on Indian peasants. Some of these land revenue policies were as follows:
• ■ Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement as a system of revenue collection in 1793 in Bengal, Bihar and Orissa.
• The land revenue settlements, regardless of the region of implementation, had certain common features. These were as follows:
• Creation of a new social class of landlords who were loyal to the British

cheatsheet: (a) Lord Cornwallis introduced a new land settlement called the Permanent Settlement; (b) Thomas Munro, the Governor of Madras (1820–27), introduced the Ryotwari Settlement.

Towards the end of the 18th century, the EEIC was no longer a mere trading company. It had been transformed into a formidable colonial power controlling most of India. In order to retain control over their colony, the British established a system of administration for the maintenance of law and order, as well as for internal security.

Indian agriculture received special attention from the EEIC. This was primarily because the main source of the state income was land revenue. Moreover, it was the aim of the British government to establish India as an agricultural supplier of cheap raw materials to industries in England. The Company tried various experiments to maximise the land revenue, regardless of its oppressive impact on Indian peasants. Some of these land revenue policies were as follows:
■ Lord Cornwallis introduced the Permanent Settlement as a system of revenue collection in 1793 in Bengal, Bihar and Orissa.
The land revenue settlements, regardless of the region of implementation, had certain common features. These were as follows:
Creation of a new social class of landlords who were loyal to the British
The Company was assured a fixed revenue or income without the trouble of collecting it from the peasants.

The early 10th century, the British Government passed the Charter Act of 1812, industry the Company was required to spend the last rupees annually on the education of Indians. The Charter Act of 1813 sparked off a debate on education across England as people argued about what policy should he implemented to educate the people of India. One group argued that the traditional learning should be imparted in classical and regional Indian languages. These were the Orientalists. On the other hand, the Anglicists, led by Thomas Macaulay, insisted that the medium of instruction should be English and the education imparted should be Western.
Lord William Beetwick finally put an end to the debate on education by passing a conclusion that defined the education policy implemented by the British in India.
Governor-Governor-Governor-Governor-Governor-G
This class was also cheap human resources the administration.

Indian industries also suffered a great setback under the British colonial rule. The British government, in the early years of the 18th century, imposed heavy duties on Indian textile imports to Britain. On the other hand, British machine-made goods entered India duty-free or at a nominal cost. The introduction of such a policy of one-way free trade made Indian goods very costly. It resulted in a significant decrease in sale for the Indian artisans and loss of their livelihoods.
##### Transport and Communication

The British realised that to consolidate their position in India, the development of proper communication facilities was necessary. They built an elaborate network of roads, canals and railway lines to connect all major cities, ports and agricultural centres. Metalled roads were built, and the railway system was introduced.
Then In 1853, the first railway line from Bombay to Thane was inaugurated. In the same year, the post and telegraph department was set up. The first telegraph line was opened from Calcutta to Agra.
One of the earliest trains in India, travelling from Bombay to Thane in 1855
critical thinking: comparative analysis, reasoning information literacy: general awareness
The British government drained the Indian economy and exploited it to the maximum. The exploitation took the following forms:

The British bought raw materials at cheap prices and sold their manufactured goods at high prices, flooding the Indian markets with their products. This led to the ruin of small-scale cottage industries.
The peasants were forced to pay high taxes, leading to increase in the poverty level and discontent. Therefore, a number of peasant revolts and uprisings took place.

In the early 19th century, the British government passed the Charter Act of 1813, whereby the Company was required to spend one lakh rupees annually on the education of Indians. The Charter Act of 1813 sparked off a debate on education across England as people argued about what policy should be implemented to educate the people of India. One group argued that the traditional learning should be imparted in classical and regional Indian languages. These were the Orientalists. On the other hand, the Anglicists, led by Thomas Macaulay, insisted that the medium of instruction should be English and the education imparted should be Western.
